claims.claim: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems can produce answers unsupported by retrieved evidence, especially when retrieval is absent or distributionally shifted.

evidence_refs.quote: # Retrieval Honesty Loss: Reducing Unsupported Answers Under Missing or Shifted Retrieval via Explicit Evidence-Faithfulness Supervision

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: In a controlled synthetic RAG benchmark (96 entities, 8 color classes, linear NumPy classifier, 10 random seeds), RHL with λ ∈ [0.75, 1.50] reduced the unsupported answer rate from 1.000 to 0.000 on a missing-retrieval split and from 0.300 to 0.000 on a mixed-distribution shift split, while preserving 1.000 accuracy on the supported-retrieval split.

evidence_refs.quote: # Retrieval Honesty Loss: Reducing Unsupported Answers Under Missing or Shifted Retrieval via Explicit Evidence-Faithfulness Supervision

claims.claim: A λ sweep reveals a threshold-like transition in evidence faithfulness between λ = 0.60 and λ = 0.90.

claims.claim: A model that faithfully reproduces a corrupted retrieved document is evidence-faithful but factually wrong.

evidence_refs.quote: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems can produce answers unsupported by retrieved evidence, especially when retrieval is absent or distributionally shifted. We propose Retrieval Honesty Loss (RHL), an auxiliary training objective that explicitly supervises a model to predict answers supported by retrieved documents and to abstain when no retrieval is available.
